text.title: AI Product Marketing Patterns Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of ai-product-marketing-patterns enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Every public claim about model behaviour is backed by an evaluable artefact (eval, benchmark, replicable demo) — not screenshots.
rule.rationale: Unverified claims violate FTC guidance and erode trust; verifiable claims become moats.

rule.statement: Trust + safety messaging is part of positioning, not an afterthought footer — especially in regulated domains.

rationale.source: First Round Review — AI product marketing essays 2025

rule.rationale: Buyers in regulated domains screen on T&S before features; treating it as legal footer loses deals.

rule.statement: Pricing narrative names the unit (per request / per token / per seat) AND its underlying cost driver.
rule.rationale: Hidden unit pricing makes prospects abandon at procurement.

rule.statement: Demos are anchored to a spec — what the demo proves the model can/cannot do — not 'cherry-picked magic'.
rule.rationale: Cherry-picked demos collapse the moment the prospect tries their own case.

rule.statement: Evals are published as marketing assets — including limitations — to differentiate from generic 'AI-powered' positioning.
rule.rationale: Limitations published in good faith build credibility; competitors who hide weaknesses look amateur.

rule.statement: If preconditions in `Applies If` are not all true, skip this methodology and route to the adjacent one referenced in Related.
rule.rationale: A skip rule keeps the decision tree closed: every branch ends at a real rule id.
